speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor's name is Alexander White. I want to back up for a minute. When you were sent to Schindler's, did you know that that's where you are going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know that you had been-- how did you know that you had been placed on the list to be able to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea that there was a list. I was notified that I'm going to go on this-- on this transport. After the war, when I found out that there was a book coming out called that Pfefferberg wrote, a neighbor of mine here, who lived in Olympia Fields right around the corner, told me he read it in the York He happen to know I was at Schindler's, so he called me up. He says, hey, there is a book coming out called I said, Schindler's list, I don't know anything about a list. He said, yes, there was supposedly a list.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I picked up the telephone. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I picked up the telephone and called up Frida in New York. I have her telephone number. She was at [INAUDIBLE]. And she worked at the enamel factory in Krakow before I came to Schindler's. So I called up there. Frida, you know, there's a book coming up, Was there something like this? She says, of course there was. I said, how was that? How come I don't know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, well, she was on the list and was taken off the list by somebody, because this Marcel Goldberg, who was making out that list with whoever else he was making it out with, was taking bribes. And she worked for Schindler's before. She was supposed to be on the list, he took her off and put somebody else on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did she get on the list? So she told me that her future husband, Kuba, who was one of the kapos in the camp, he was one of the-- of the supervisors, you know, a nice guy, Kuba Deutsche. He-- he was her boyfriend. He went with her to see Marcel Goldberg. And she started crying to him that she had lost her family. And [INAUDIBLE] she had worked for Schindler's all the time. And she was put back on the list. Now, I asked her, how did I get the list? She says, it was mazel, luck. And my neighbor asked me, what do you think? How did you get on the list? I said, it wasn't luck. I'll tell you how I think I got on the list. The people that were at Schindler's were favorite people. They did not have the trades that they were listed. You see? For example, one guy with me there was a teacher, who was listed as a glazier. All the others who were listed as something, that they work at machine, engineer, machine bow. This one was a carpenter, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to understand that when the Germans used to come, for example, we were in the ghetto, or [INAUDIBLE]. Say the German Wehrmacht came, or the air force, and they wanted, say, five carpenters, six glaziers. Every Jew all of a sudden became a carpenter and a glazier. He had no idea what carpentry is all about, all glazing is all about, but he hoped that one of the guys will know how to do carpentering and glazing, so he will learn from him. And he will somewhere, somehow, finagle, you know. And that's what happened there. I said, they needed one real one. And I happened to be the real one. That is my feeling. I was the youngest amongst the glaziers, you see, in Krakow, in Plaszow. So they probably selected me, because they needed one that knows how to do glazing. And that's how I think I got there, you know. And there, as I told you, the rations were very meager. You know, it was hard to survive on those rations initially, until Mr. Schindler arranged with Countess Daubek. There was a German countess who owned a flour mill across the road from where that factory was. And they arranged where our rations then improved. And we got then one sixth off a loaf of bread, instead of one eighth. And every so often, we would get a quarter of a loaf of bread. Now, I happen to be in particularly-- particularly lucky there. Because where the glazing room was a sort aside from the camp, from the main factory. It was in the corner. There was no window. So it was steel doors. It was locked. No one even knew there was anything there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there I was, and a fellow together with me there, and there happened to be a hole in the wall. And on the other side of the wall was a textile factory, because that entire factory was a textile factory. Part of it was given to Schindler, you see, and I happen to be right at that wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume that one of the leather belts must've gone through that hole for the machinery. So when we got there, the guy lifts me up there. And I go up there, and I peek into the factory there. And there were the Czechs. And they saw me. The following day, there was a piece of bread. The next day, there was a piece of meat. The next day, there was speck, you know, bacon. The next day, there were a few potatoes-- all kinds, every so often. So we used to divide it, this other guy who was there with me, Mr. Miller, and myself. We would divide it amongst ourselves. And I would divide it and share it with Frida, Sonia, and Mr. Gross, you know. And whenever Frida see us, when we went to her wedding, she tells my wife, you know, how I used to bring a potato. And I used to divide it. And we all ate. So I had some-- I luckily had some extra rations. And there are some other things. For example, I wanted to somewhere, somehow, to give them a gift or something. I didn't know what to do. Now, we had glass. So what I did, I cut little pieces of glass, you know. And I made, like, little stands for-- for-- for photographs to put in. And this Gross, he was an engineer before the war. And he was in charge at the camp, you know, in the factory in engineering. So he made for me little metal screws and other things. And he made me a drill. I told him how to make a drill out of a triangular file, you know, with a tip. And I made these holes. And I put it in. And I put it into that hole in the wall. And they would pick that up, you know. And then, they would give me-- and I don't know what they did with it. They probably threw it out. Because it was sharp, and I just took a stone, you know, and smoothed the edges with a regular stone, you know, with a little water. And I was smoothing  the edges. But anyway, so I had these extra rations. And then, of course, at the end of the war on May 8, you know, the Russians came in. And we were liberated. And as we were liberated, you know, many of the young people went around to German houses and tried to take stuff, et cetera, et cetera. You know, I couldn't stand it. I went when I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I-- I was very upset, because I told them, how can you possibly go to their house and take away stuff? You know, you don't even know who this man was. He could've been one of the good guys. I couldn't. It was awful. It was awful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I tried to take-- make my way back home. And that is a story by itself that will take a long time for me to tell you. I was sitting on the roofs of-- of trains, you know. The bridges were bombed. We had to stop at the river, cross the river, then take another train and so on. And before we left, we were given at Schindler's some materials-- you see, at Schindler's stocked up with all kinds of materials from the withdrawing troops. And he had-- so I got material for a suit. And I got a little Hitler-- Hitler Youth blouse and Hitler Youth hat. You know, and that's what I wore. And I went back. My first step was to go back to Poland. And I remember, we arrived in-- finally arrived in Prague. And the first hot soup was given by an American Red Cross kettle. Since that time, I always donate to the American Red Cross, because that was the first real hot soup that I ate. Although, I had hot soup after liberation with some of the guys who were butchers at Schindler's. And they took away some sows from whatever. And they butcher it, so they now got us some good food. But that was impressed me tremendously. And I came back to Poland to my hometown. And there, I started looking for who survived, you know. And there was a lady who survived, Mrs. Kaub. She was hidden by a Belorussian, she and her daughter. And her story, when I listen to her story-- how she survived, and how that Belorussian was beaten by the guards to tell where she's-- he's hiding the Jewess-- I mean, it was awful. He didn't give her away, you know. And then, hid her in the forest. In the winter, made a hole, et cetera. Brought her food. And then, the kid developed high fever and rheumatic fever-- her-- the little girl, her daughter. And how he took her back and said, I don't care what happens to me. You are staying in my house, and this and that. And so she was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took everyone who came back. I came back first. Then one day, I came to her room, and she says, guess who is here? Black Jo is here. The [NON-ENGLISH] is here. He came from Auschwitz, you see. Then, a week later my uncle comes from Buchenwald-- my father's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father had two brothers and a sister. They all perished with their families. One brother, [? Yaso ?] had six children. They all perished. And the sister had children. They all perished. But this uncle that was in Buchenwald survived. You know, he came back. He came back from the west. And he said, look, you're not going to stay here. He brought American cigarettes, chocolate, all kinds of things. Because I wanted to start seeing what I can do, start schooling whatever, you know. And he said, no you are going back with me. And what we did is, we dug out some silver that we had buried. My uncle's silver, we had buried at a place. And there was furniture that was given to a doctor there, a Ukrainian doctor, who was a friend of the family, you know. And he took it, and he gave us some money. After the war, you know, we came there. And we bought American scripts that many returnees from the west who came back-- Poles used to work there, you know-- came back. We bought their scripts. And we placed it into a double valise. You know, we made a double bottom. That was my uncle's, and he had two friends of his who survived with him from Buchenwald from his town. And they-- we put all this money in there. And we marched off. My father bribed a guy from his hometown to give us a permission to cross the border 15 kilometers into Czechoslovakia, you see, because we were right near the Czechoslovak border. And we hired a horse buggy. All we had to do was give them a package of cigarettes, American cigarettes. And my father had-- my uncle had the American cigarettes. So we went over there. And we came to Bardejov in Slovakia. And there was a Jewish family who were wine dealers. They survived in hiding. And they were distant relatives of ours, of my mother's. We stayed there. The following day or two days later, we're going to go to the train to go to the west-- to go to the west. The police picks up at the train. They claimed that we are Polish spies. So now, they throw us in jail. So there, my uncle and I sit in the-- in the Czech jail. And they take a whole report-- how and what. Finally, some with how, they decided we are going to be shipped back to Poland. We are shipped back to Poland. And a Czech policeman is guarding us. My uncle takes a package of American cigarettes, gives them to the policeman. The policeman said, he's going look the other way. And we turned around and went now to Kosice, Kaschau, which was also Czechoslovakia, but a bigger town. There we went on the train. And we went to the west. We come to the border of demarcation line between the American occupation zone, and the Soviet occupation zone. Now, Russian officers and-- and Czech officers want to see our papers. There was me, my cousin from Auschwitz, my uncle, and these two friends of my uncles from his town who survived and were also coming back. And we all had our monies stuck between in the double bottom. Now, they come along. They want to see papers. I was lucky, because I had from the camp-- from Brunnlitz, Schindler's camp-- before we left we got the certificate in Czech and in German that we were prisoners at camp, in Brunnlitz [? Brishow. ?] So they saw [INAUDIBLE] is a Czech name. They let me go-- continue on the train. See, my uncle had from Landsberg, which was a DP camp in Germany. They let him go. My cousin and the two guys, they took off. So the guys left their luggage on the train. We come to the border, which was a real border between Czechoslovakia and West Germany. We come to that border, and we go down. You know, go down the train. That's the end station. And there are Czech policeman there waiting. And they take us into a room. They wanted our names, et cetera. And the Czech takes a knife, and just goes like this, opens the double bottom, shakes all the money out, starts counting the money, puts it in a pile, has me and my uncle sign, and left the leaves up there. Now, we don't know what's going to be. It was after midnight. You know, it's dark. We don't know. Just my uncle and I sit there at the guard post. Nobody's around, not a soul, nothing. I tell my uncle, I said, look, [NON-ENGLISH], we have got to make [HEBREW], that is in Hebrew, run away. I mean, we're not going to sit here. Since this is the end station-- this is the guard house-- the West Germany must be over there. Let's walk. So we walked. We walked and walked. We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we see a German. The Germans had to wear armbands, like the Jews had to now in Czechoslovakia. There's a German there. And I ask him in German, said, where's the border? Where is West Germany? He says, you were just there. You're back in Czechoslovakia. You were just there. So I had a gold watch that I got back from home. I was so thankful to him, I gave him the gold watch, believe it or not, to the German, you know. And he showed us where to go. And we crossed the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I'm not going to tell you the whole story, because it's a long story to tell you. But when I came to that camp that my uncle was in, OK, these guys weren't there. You know, the guys that were taking off, you know. The guy's come, and they claim that I stole their money. The story-- I'm giving them a cock and bull story. They believe my uncle, but they think I have the money somewheres. They want me to go with them to the rabbi-- I said, I'm no going to any rabbi-- and to swear by the Bible that I don't have their money. I could have killed those son of a guns, you know. I went to the rabbi. Didn't hear.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to know in Munich, two years later, my uncle comes to visit me. I was in medical school. Comes to visit me, and he says, he wants me to sign a paper. I said, why? He said there are two guys who want to know, is Bialywlos, Samuel and Alexander there? They have money that the Czechs took away from them. You know, they can get it back, provided they take 50%. See? So [DRINKS] since it was all in my and my uncle's name, their money, I said, I ain't signing nothing, unless these guys give half of the money to charity. Because they accused me-- dragged me to the rabbi, et cetera. And to make a long story short, I signed it. I got $50, OK, for this stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I was coming to the United States, I guarded those $50 with my life. I kept it in my wallet. Before I was going to come to the States, I was going to buy myself something. So there was a Jewish fellow who was a black marketeer on the [INAUDIBLE]. That's where the black market was in Munich. I-- He tells me, go and see this and this guy. He will give you a good, you know, many German marks, more than normally. I go there. I give him those $50. And he takes his nail and shoves his finger under the zero, and pulls off the zero. What these two schnooks did is, they gave me a $5 bill-- I didn't know dollars, you know-- a $5 bill and had pasted on a zero next to it. So now I had $5. That's what I came to America with, with the $5. I'm going to stop here, because I say, I mean, there's no end to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I thank you very much for sharing your story with us. And I wish we had more time. But we'll show your photos and bring your family in. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It's a pleasure. Thanks for coming. This picture is my sister and a number of her friends from [? Beit ?] [? Yakov, ?] which was a Hebrew school. My sister is the second from left with that-- with the bag that you can see there. It was done, approximately, I would say about 1938. I received this picture from my cousin in Israel who survived in Siberia. So I don't know exactly the date, but approximately 1938. This is my uncle Abraham, my mother's brother, who perished with his entire family. He was the father of the white Jo. And he had another son, white Aaron, and two daughters Rachel and Sarah. They all perish together with the first so-called resettlement in 1942. This is my cousin Jo, the black Jo, who just passed away about a year ago. And he was a locksmith. He survived Auschwitz. And that's him showing before the war. This is my-- the town of my birth, Krosno in southern Poland. And these are the arches at the central square. Most of these homes were owned by Jews. The Jews lived the right in the square in the town. And most non-Jews lived usually outside town. And this doesn't do it any justice, because this was taken during the Communist regime. It was prettier, I thought, before the war. This is a picture of the Jewish sports club in Krosno. And two of my cousins are on that picture. The second one from the left, David, the tool and die maker who perished with his little boy and my brother in Auschwitz. And the fourth girl from the right side with the dark hair, Malka who was shot by the Gestapo after a woman who hid her took her money away and delivered her to the Gestapo. This is a picture again of friends from the sports club. And where you see the x's on their hats is my cousin that you saw in the other picture, and my-- Malka, the other cousin on a ski outing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the cousin on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, cousin on the right, the second one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David. And Malka is the fifth one from the right. This is one page of the list which lists my name from Schindler's list, which I received from the Wiesenthal Museum. On top, you will see KL, which stands for [GERMAN], concentration camp. Gross-Rosen, we were under that camp, as your heard in my story, that we stopped over there on the way to Schindler's camp, Gross-Rosen. [GERMAN] which means war camp, Brunnlitz, that is the place where Schindler's factory was. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means this list of the male prisoners 8, 13th, the 4th, which means April 13, 1945. [GERMAN], which-- page six. And here, you can see my name, Bialywlos-- which stands for white hair in Polish-- Alexander, born, 4, 6, which means April 6, 1923. And there it says glazier-- glaser. This is a picture that was taken when we visited Mrs. Schindler when she came to New Jersey. And she stayed at the house of this gentleman, an engineer who had married Schindler's secretary. Now, Mrs. Schindler is in the first row to the left. The Schindler's secretary is the second lady. Above her is her husband who is an engineer, and he survived the-- Schindler's camp. I am to the left of him, the first one on the left. The next one is Dr. Mandel, who was Professor of Medicine at the University-- State University in New York. And he is originally from Vienna, where he was a professor of medicine. His wife, and the last one on the right side is my wife, Inez.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll ask you to introduce who's here with you today. Can you introduce who's here for us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you be a little louder?

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you introduce your family to us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To my left is my dear wife, Inez. As you can see, she is beautiful, in body and soul. And our oldest daughter, Denise. And our son, Les. And here, I have a picture of Julie, who is in California. She's not here. And this is the only picture I have that is recent, and when she was in the Navy, stationed at the Gulf. She was a lieutenant in the Navy. She's a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to say to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that I'm extremely lucky, not only to be alive and be here, but to have such a lovely family that I love dearly. And I hope they stay healthy and well, and they continue the legacy I got from my father, which-- who told me be a mensch. So if they stay menschen, I'll be very happy, and I'll die in peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you'd like to say to your husband or to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we love-- we love him. And we're glad we're all together, except for Julie not being here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much.
